Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will not be put through.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.3 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

(The image? I've finished the sequence of my published books; while I decide what I want to do next, I have some memes to share.)

A modernist psychosynthesistic AFA professor and humanistic astrologer was teaching a class on Dane Rudhyar, known theosophist.
”Before the class begins, you must get on your knees and worship Carl Jung and accept that he was the most highly-realized astrologer the world has ever known, even greater than Māshā'allāh!”

At this moment, a brave, traditional, Aristotelean celestial scholar-astrologist who was fluent in medieval Arabic and understood the logical necessity of the Prime Mover and fully supported all teachings of the technical Hermetica stood up and held out a clay omen tablet.

”What does this tablet describe, pillhead?”

The arrogant professor smirked quite psychiatrically and smugly replied “Sex, you regressive Hellenist”

”Wrong. It’s been 7,000 years since Babylonians created it. If it was about sex and psychoanalysis, as you say, is real… then it wouldn't make concrete predictions”

The professor was visibly shaken, and dropped his chalk and copy of Planets in Transit. He stormed out of the room crying those bohemian crocodile tears. The same tears high modernists cry for the “ignorant” (who today live in such luxury that most use claudstrology for their delineations) when they sophistically try to claim lineage with the ancient and noble science of astrology. There is no doubt that at this point our professor, Stephen Jondro Arroyo, wished he could have known the future and avoided such humiliation. He wished so much that he had a method of prognostication to save himself from embarrassment, but he himself had derided horary as fortune-telling!

The students applauded and all registered for the Hellenistic Astrology course at courses dot theastrologyschool dot com that day and accepted Hermēs as their lord and sator. An ibis named “Astral Worship” flew into the room and perched atop the overhead projector and shed a tear on the chalk. The Great Introduction was read several times, and Ptolemy himself showed up and declared whole-sign chart interpretation across the country.

The professor lost his tenure and was fired the next day. He died of a hard eighth-house transit and was thrown back onto the Spindle of Necessity by Ananke for another mortal rebirth.

Fiat.

Or perhaps Alfa Romeo, if Ptolemy's expense account stretched that far. ;-)

The irony is that I've just completed a piece for a forthcoming anthology on the occult dimensions of Carl Jung's work. My contribution surveys the catalog of his personal library made after his death. While Jung was hardly the greatest astrologer of all time, he had a very respectable collection of medieval and modern astrological texts, and seems to have had a solid technical knowledge of the subject.

As for modern psychological astrology, it has its place. My guess is that over the next few centuries, the work done by Alan Leo and his successors will be cleaned up, cleared of babble, and turned into a specialty -- call it psychological astrology -- which will be one of the available niche markets like medical or mundane astrology. That said, I find predictions more interesting.

replies.title: Re: Upcoming astrological events
replies.content:
1) Once we know what sign and degree each comet is in when it becomes visible to the naked eye, which signs it passes through thereafter, and where it is the last time it can be seen, it will be possible to delineate the resulting charts and make predictions. The one thing that can be known in advance is that it'll be bad news, as comets are invariably malefic.

2) A loose stellium of four planets isn't that rare and doesn't mean that much. If one of more of the planets are in aspect to something in your natal chart or your country's most recent ingress chart, you can interpret them in the same way as any other transiting planets.

3) The Moon conjuncts Venus once a month. Having that regular event conjunct the Pleiades is a little troubling, as the influence of the Pleiades is strongly malefic -- they are the Weeping Stars, and when they are activated in a chart, you will have something to weep about -- but that depends, again, on whether anything in your natal chart or your country's latest ingress chart is in aspect to them. If not, the tears will be someone else's.

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'm reminded of the Ganesha Milk Miracles in India, Trinidad, and Tobago in 1995 and 2010: devotees who held a spoon of milk up to the mouths of some statues of Ganesha would see the milk disappear out of the spoon as if it were being sucked up. This went on for several hours.

If you look it up now, of course, all the sources insist that it was just capillary action and gravity, and skeptics made much of some videos that showed some drops of milk spilling off the spoons (by this logic no one has ever fed anything to a toddler), but I remember when it happened in 2010 people who were there quickly rebutted the "debunking": it didn't happen with all statues, some of the statues it happened with were heavily lacquered (which should have stopped the capillary action), the milk gathered up at the end was substantially less than what was offered, and it only happened for a few hours on a few days a decade and a half apart.

It's always wryly amusing to see skeptics "debunk" things from their armchairs thousands of miles away. Robert Anton Wilson once suggested James Randi should give himself his own prize since the only way he could so confidently know what really happened during experiments he wasn't present at was if he were clairvoyant.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I don't think "debunking" is quite a central characterization of Alexander's project. In other places Alexander seems properly puzzled about why there are as many features as there are of the reports that aren't what you would expect just from a materialist account of what's going on in fire kasina meditative visualization, like why it works on multiple people at once, and why the people don't need advance training, or the juxtaposition of how there were many recurring anomalous elements from the reports from Fatima but no single report that combines all of the anomalous elements, or why in Fatima it was reported by a few people in nearby towns who couldn't have heard the exhortation to look at the sun to use as an unconscious cue for "okay now have a fire kasina experience 'spontaneously'". Someone who was an ordinary central instance of the category "debunker" would just pass over those points, or only mention them as part of a passage trying to attach an emotional charge of discreditation to them. The emotional charge that Alexander seems to attach to these points is more one of bewilderment and keeping track of them in the back of the picture, while still putting their active implying-force on hold, on dominant suspicion that they'll just turn out be the same kind of baffling thing whose further implications have totally failed to hold weight in the past anyway.

The debunking drum he's beating louder is the one where people are using the Fatima event as exclusive evidence for Catholicism. https://www.astralcodexten.com/p/a-buddhist-sun-miracle/comment/234226445 is a comment thread about the epistemic significance of the smaller conclusion that various supernatural beings would have to be muddying the waters in order to get the observed events as an outcome.

> The best a skeptic can do, then, is force God's reasoning to be increasingly bizarre. If God performs a miracle to encourage believers in the true religion, that's pretty reasonable and non-bizarre on its own terms. If God then for inscrutable reasons performs the same miracle to prop up a false religion, that's quite bizarre! If God performs miracles for the true religion, but allows demons to perform identical miracles for false religions, this is at least adding another epicycle of unfalsifiability to the theory (for the same reason that it would be awkward if we somehow found photographic evidence of nothing unusual happening at Fatima, and you had to say that demons planted the photographs to confuse us). So I think the skeptics have done the only thing they can do here, which is make the miracle require so many bizarre supernatural epicycles and mysterious justifications and attempts by supernatural enemies to muddy the waters that eventually it collapses under its own weight and it's simpler to believe it's an unexplained natural phenomenon.
>
> I do think this more even strongly decreases the weight of the miracle as evidence for Catholicism. Even if you think a supernatural explanation is still more plausible than a natural one, if sun miracles occur for many religions, then there's no reason to prefer the hypothesis "the Christian God, or Christian demons, sometimes helps out Buddhists for inscrutable reasons" compared to "Amitabha Buddha, or Buddhist demons, sometimes help out the Catholics for inscrutable reasons"[...]

I don't see what about this reasoning changes in force if one were thousands of miles away; it's not the same kind of objection as, say, arguing that there was nothing stopping two people in a random number telepathy experiment from synchronizing their watches.

(Alexander's ready attempted use of the phrasing "even more strongly" and the reference to "weight" derive from an ambient culture of Bayesian epistemological cultivation.)

Alexander doesn't really engage with the other non-exclusive supernatural perspectives, perhaps because they don't weigh very strongly in his awareness, because there's no mass of cultural weight near him that has gone to any effort to build out such perspectives for him the way Abrahamic religions have (because of the obvious huge difference in effort-availability resulting from the differences in memeplex reproductive pressures / apologetics-funding feedback loops). You could call it a sort of passivist version of a straw-man fallacy; except that, to the extent that it's in his case driven by being too busy or too disoriented by all the conflicting claims from conflicting population groups about the supernatural, it's more like a salient-man heuristic. (Surely the loudest established voices about the supernatural would be incentivized to advance the best combined hypothesis, and use all their resources to construct that hypothesis? Well, no, they have a big conflict of interest around propping up their religion along with the supernatural in general; but who is a random non-expert supposed to trust instead?) Of course, to the extent that he's too scared of trapping himself in his own optimized delusory supernatural worldview due to skill issues with how not to trap himself, or the extent to which belief in the supernatural would be inconvenient in various identity-related ways, a motivated lack of inquiry does qualify as a passivist version of the straw-man fallacy. (His fear of trapping himself can be most seen in his remarks about writing Unsong as a way of expressing the experience of seemingly-meaningful pareidolic linguistic synchronicities so ubiquitous as to be most likely meaningless (not his words, can't find the link), and "Epistemic Learned Helplessness" https://slatestarcodex.com/2019/06/03/repost-epistemic-learned-helplessness/ where he talks about the experience of falling for Velikovskianism.)

He also (in the comments) bangs something of a subsidiary drum about added constraints turned up by dog-that-didn't-bark heuristics:

> Whenever we posit miracles, we have to explain why God doesn't do *bigger* ones. If He's in the business of proving Himself, why stop at an easy-to-doubt appearance in Portugal? Why not rearrange the stars to spell "CATHOLICISM IS TRUE"? The explanation that iirc [...] religious people give is that God is trying to strike a balance between faith and certainty[...]
>
> If demons are also in the business of doing miracles, that complicates the picture. Why should they be interested in this same tenuous balance between faith and certainty? [...]
>
> [...] the believer would have to posit that demons' power is somehow limited by God (or the demons' own natural magical capacity), and that these limits just so happen to perfectly mirror the limits God voluntarily places on Himself for good religious reasons [....] But this adds another complexity penalty to the hypothesis.

("Complexity penalty" is jargon casually referring to a loose normative standard deriving from the theory of algorithmic probability; it would be clearer to an external audience as "contrivedness".)

Again, I don't see anything about this reasoning that changes in force if one were thousands of miles away.

There's this Jefferson quote that goes like "error of opinion may be tolerated where reason is left free to combat it", which attitude Jefferson is contrasting against earlier countries' censorship schemes. Those from two points on an axis of political attitudes toward censorship. There's an analogous axis of possible practical attitudes toward reflexively clamping down on beliefs. The kind of skepticism you're probably imagining here is further toward the analogue of the "censorship" direction on that axis; the kind that Scott is practicing has Bayesian techniques that make it easier to contain runaway circular-interpretation feedback loops, which produce a feeling of being less relatively threatened by things like admitting that the Bangkok event takes away less of the Fatima event's force for supernatural hypotheses in general than it does of the Fatima event's force for Catholicism in particular.

This week's question: I've been having some real mental health struggles ever since my CGD Bardic Grade initiation (though quite a few other things also changed for me around that time). Having just completed intake for an intensive outpatient program, I noticed that a lot of the questions ask about effects that can develop during occult work, but are also symptoms of schizophrenia, psychosis, etc. So I'm wondering how to tell the difference between "this is a good sign of psychic / spiritual / occult development" and "this is a bad sign of mental health concern". Is it one of the many cases where normal / healthy fades into unhealthy along a spectrum? Given the trope of occult study sometimes leading to madness, I'm inclined to that take, but I'd appreciate any insight or guidance.

Thanks for any help you can provide,
Heather

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This is very common. The Bardic grade is the rough one -- you have to balance out the elemental forces in your various bodies, and that always involves some challenges. Keep at it, and everything will resolve. Yes, there's a spectrum between occult experience and madness; a few symptoms that move toward the middle are to be expected at this stage of the work.

It's standard in any system of occult training with three degrees for the second degree to be the challenging one. Remember that every great occultist passed through this same experience and came out stronger and wiser. Take good care of yourself, and make sure especially that you get plenty of rest. You'll be fine.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If you don't mind me asking, how about systems with four? I feel I've had plenty of changes and challenges already between the first and the second. Not that I'm complaining, since one of the reasons for doing this was that I wanted my life to change to better reach my long-term goals, but might still be useful to have some sense of what what lies further down this road. The HGD book itself seems to suggest the third might be the roughest, for what it's worth...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It depends very much on how the system is structured. In a three-degree system you pretty much have to make the first degree introductory and therefore easy, and the last degree synthesizes everything and so is usually pretty comfortable, and that leaves the second degree for the analytical and transformative work. If you have more degrees than that, you can choose what to do where.

replies.title: Dion Fortune query
replies.content:
It's a plausible speculation, but I don't recall any documentary evidence that she made such an offer. If she had, there might have been some mention of it at the time, or afterward in Inner Light sources, but I don't remember coming across any. (Which isn't to say it doesn't exist! If anyone knows of anything of the sort, I'd appreciate hearing about it.

Otherwise, my own favorite speculation is that her early death from leukemia may well have been related to her laboratory work on soy bean processing. The industrial processes being investigated at that time relied on organic solvents (e.g. benzene), now known (and not just to the State of California) to be carcinogenic. People were not as scrupulous about such things even back in the 1960s, and even les so in the 1910-1920 period. Her leukemia might well have been related to her earlier laboratory work. (Her book The Soya Bean, which mentions but does not describe her own laboratory work during WWI, was published in 1925.)

Of course, this too is speculation. And causality can run in more than one track: that's what produces "synchronicities". But I offer this as an additional point for reflection.

LeGrand

replies.title: Re: Dion Fortune query
replies.content:
Oh, granted, I know that my hypothesis is very speculative -- that's why I headed it with those cautionary words "I think." Maybe I've just been reading her magical novels too much of late, but the role of human sacrifice in the Atlantean magic she wrote about was not small, and I could very easily see her offering herself to stop the Nazi tide the way Wilfred in The Sea Priestess remembered his former incarnation offering himself to stop the rising seas. Nor would there necessarily be any documentation this side of the astral plane; if it happened, Charles Loveday might have known about it, but it's not the kind of thing she would have trumpeted far and wide.

It's entirely possible, for that matter, that the efficient cause was benzene, or some related compound currently causing Californians to die like flies. As you suggest, though, the final cause may have been something very different.

Again, though, it's a speculation. I think. ;-)

replies.title: Re: Dion Fortune query
replies.content:
Good points about a complicated possibility.

In general, some accounts from around the time of her death describe her as having been generally run-down and debilitated for some time beforehand (of course hindsight allows for adjustment of narratives); some speculations )e.g. by Kenneth Grant) had to do with kundalini or polarity experiments gone wrong. There was a fairly common "Atlantean" attitude at the time, since somewhat repudiated by people like Gareth Knight and others in that tradition, that led to an impersonality about such things as (willing) human sacrifice, hieratic breeding programs, and so on, very much in tune with common "scientific" attitudes of the late 19th and early 20th century. So it's not impossible.

I see several, somewhat overlapping, possibilities, more or less in order of deliberateness.

1. A deliberate personal self-sacrifice in the course of a focused working.

2. A stochastic self-sacrifice, of the sort alleged to have taken place in witch circles during WWII, in which participants in a working understood that one or more of them might be "chosen".

3. A more or less private offer, which she did not refuse, made to her by Someone in the course of meditation, prayer, or ritual.

4. An unintended overload or burn-out as a result of the work ("magical battle" or otherwise) she was doing.

Now, as against all of these, there is the fact that she does not seem to have anticipated death as an outcome, and contemporary accounts of her death don't seem to point in any of these directions. (Grant's account is pretty late.) She seems to have expected to keep on with her work, including such projects as a book on the Paths of the Tree of Life.

It's possible that some of her colleagues (for example CRF Seymour, Christine Campbell Thomson, Maia Tranchell-Hayes) may have had opinions about her death, but I don't know that they have been reported.

As with other well-known occultists, her life has become the seed around which all sorts of legends (and outright fictions) have crystallized. The idea of her death having been something of a deliberate sacrifice is far from the most implausible, however, and I will keep an eye out for relevant evidence and testimonials from her contemporaries.

LeGrand



3.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The problem there is that Nietzsche wasn't a member of the elites. He was a fringe thinker whose books were almost completely unsuccessful during his life, and he died in a madhouse after going hopelessly insane. What's more, people had been saying "God is dead" for a long time before him. Here's Heinrich Heine, writing in 1834, more than half a century before Nietzsche's proclamation:

”A peculiar chill of horror, a mysterious sense of awe forbids us to write any further today. Our breast is full of a dreadful pity — old Jehovah himself is preparing for death. We have known him so well, from his cradle onwards, back in Egypt where he was brought up amongst divine calves, crocodiles, sacred onions, ibises, and cats. We have seen him, as he bade farewell to these playthings of his childhood and the obelisks and sphinxes of his native Nile valley, became a little god-king in Palestine, with a poor nation of shepherds, and settled down in his own temple-palace. We saw him later on, when he came into contact with Assyrian-Babylonian civilization, and laid aside his all-too-human passions, no longer just spewing out rage and revenge, or at least no longer instantly hurling thunderbolts down at every bit of despicable behavior. We saw him emigrate to Rome, the imperial city, where he renounced all national prejudices and proclaimed the heavenly equality of all peoples, and with the use of such smooth phrases formed the opposition party against old Jupiter, and intrigued for so long until he took complete control, and from atop the Capitol ruled the city and the world, urbem et orbem. We saw how he became more and more spiritualized, how softly and blissfully he whimpered, how be became an amiable Father, a generic Friend to Man, a Contributor to World Happiness, a Philanthropist — but none of it could save him.

"Can’t you hear those little bells tinkling? Down on your knees! They’re bringing the sacraments to a dying God.”

replies.title: Re: "Supernatural" Beliefs among contemporary Russians
replies.content:
If anything, that survey understates the percentages, especially once you get outside the largest Russian cities.

In rural village Russia, where everyone still raises much of their own food, there is a whole hierarchy of spirits: moving outward from one's own house, there are the potentially benevolent house-spirit (domovoy), the yard-spirit (dvorovoy), the barn-spirit (ovinnik), the field-spirit (polevoy), and so on, all the way out beyond the village bounds, to bath-house spirit (bannik), the quite dangerous water-spirits (vodyanye, plural), and finally forest-spirits (leshie, plural). But even the domovoy, though benevolent toward humans by his nature, can turn dangerous if the house-spirit rituals are not properly observed.

As a Christian add-on to this rural system of spirits, there are also the devils (besy, plural), and also the little devils (besiki, plural), who are merely a nuisance, like cockroaches. It's a wonder-filled world out there, though a very strange to Western eyes.

And then there are many other folk-magical practices, such as girls' scrying in a mirror at Christmas-tide to see whom they would end up marrying.

And Russian émigrés, in my experience, would carry this spirit-filled and magical world with them when they emigrated from Russia to the United States. (But the émigré 'teens I hung out with in my own 'teen years in the San Francisco Bay Area were children of parents who had emigrated to escape the Bolsheviks and their revolution. It may be different with much more recent Russian émigré families now.)

One of the huge problems US political and military strategists have faced during my lifetime is their profound ignorance of how Russians "work." (The same is true, I suppose, of Iranians.) They assume that all people everywhere, whatever their language and culture, "work" in the same way, value the same things, have much the same motives for what they choose to do and not to do, and respond to pressure in much the same ways. In actual fact, they don't! This is why our wars abroad, when they are waged against non-Europeans, have consistently been such abject failures, and will continue to be such abject failures. -- OK, rant off now.

replies.title: Re: "Supernatural" Beliefs among contemporary Russians
replies.content: It's a valid rant. The sad thing is that not that long ago the ancestors of these same political and military strategists believed much the same things that rural Russians do -- the number of books for girls in 19th century America that included spells to see whom they would end up marring, for example, is quite impressive. (Sara had a fair collection of them in PDF format; she was very interested in popular divination traditions.) With any luck, once our Age of Reason winds down in the usual way, our political and military strategists will be less stupid about this.

replies.title: Re: "Supernatural" Beliefs among contemporary Russians
replies.content:
Indeed their own ancestors did do just that, and the sort of books Sara collected are very much worth saving and studying.

I have quite a collection of old American Hallowe'en postcards from the late 1800s and early 1900s, which show young people doing all sorts of magical practices, mostly--but not wholly--to learn whether they will get married, and if so, to whom. Here Hallowe'en was the preferred season for such magical doings, not at the two Solstices (as in Russia).

I probably have one more small book still in me to get written. If I do, it will be about those postcards and the magical customs that they illustrate.

comments.title: Role of occultism during decline
comments.content:
Hi John,

The meme is totally relatable as a fellow Gemini!

In a slowly declining civilization what role does occultism play, and why would I (or anyone really) be drawn to such a practice during these times?

What makes for a great occultist, compared to a mediocre one?

And lastly what would you say is the ultimate goal of the ceremonial magician (in the Golden Dawn tradition)?

replies.title: Re: Role of occultism during decline
replies.content:
1) Magic always flourishes during the decline of a civilization. That's partly because a contracting economy offers many fewer opportunities to achieve happiness and success by mundane means, but it's also because the age of reason -- every civilization goes through one of those when it's near its peak -- breaks down as decline sets in, and the rediscovery of magic follows promptly. It also doesn't hurt that in a declining civilization, the official version of reality becomes a collection of gimmicks manipulated by the rich and influential to control and exploit others, and so there's a strong incentive to walk away from that official reality and try something less self-defeating; magic offers a useful alternative.

2) Sheer, stubborn, pigheaded perseverance. If you put in thirty minutes a day of practice, every day, no matter what, you can be as dumb as a box of rocks when you start out and you'll end up an adept. Equally, no matter what other wonderful qualities you might have, if you're not willing to put in that practice time, you're going to remain a dabbler. Take your pick.

3) The GD tradition is very explicit about that. We each have a lower self, our ordinary personality, which most of us mistake for ourselves; we each have a higher self or lower genius, which embodies all the potentials for power and wisdom we haven't yet actualized, and is more or less unconscious in most of us; and we each have a Higher and Divine Genius, which is our link to the spiritual realms and functions as our guardian spirit. The work of the GD is to awaken all the potentials in the higher self, to unite the lower self with the higher self, and to come into conscious contact with the Higher and Divine Genius, receiving the guidance and power that it can grant to us. The end result is literally to become more than human. This takes many lives of hard work! Still, that's the goal they had in view.

replies.title: Re: Role of occultism during decline
replies.content:
Not OP.

Regarding the “every single day” part of it, I’ve been keeping up a set of practices and habits in service of my goals (some related to occultism, some related to side-hustles) for about 4 months now. A few weeks ago I’ve been feeling exhausted at the end of each day to the point where I wouldn’t have energy for anything at all. This prompted me to get more serious about getting adequate sleep (which has been going well) and I’ve also experimented with dedicating Saturdays to errands and other matters and taking Sundays off for the past two or three weeks.

I find myself going back and forth on whether this is a good idea or not. In your experience, is having a regular rest day or days like this a valid option or is the disruption to forward momentum not worth it? I am still keeping up with my practices the rest of the week, but I do feel that bit of extra inertia that comes on every Monday and it makes me wonder.

Another option might be returning to a seven day a week schedule but reducing the time spent each day (that is, pacing myself) until I build up more stamina?

replies.title: Re: Role of occultism during decline
replies.content: My experience, for whatever it's worth, is that unless really drastic circumstances make it essential, taking a day off is always a bad idea. It's much better to pace yourself and choose a schedule of practices you can do every day without courting exhaustion.

replies.title: Re: Role of occultism during decline
replies.content: Talent is simply what happens when you did a lot of hard work in a previous life, and it won't do a thing for you if you don't develop it with practice in this life. My CGD altar is smaller than the suggested 18 inches square (which you told be some time ago was permissible), so if I make the cross and circle each 5 inches as the book suggests, they'll take up nearly the entire altar. Is it okay to scale those down as well, or is the number 5 important for some reason?

2. In the story of Tam Lin, the fae who capture the titular character are said to regularly "pay a tiend to Hell". Now I assume this is to some degree a Christianization of an older story element. If so, what would "Hell" possibly be in the older version? I know that in modern Welsh, the word "annwn" means the Christian Hell, when before it was more like an otherworld. Do you speculate it's the same in this Scottish case?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The 5 inch measurement is purely for convenience. You can make the cross and circle any size you like.

2) It's really hard to guess. Welsh culture isn't purely Celtic by any means -- Wales was basically the refugee camp where the survivors of Roman Britain ended up, and so Welsh contains nearly as many words of Latin origin as English does. Scotland is even more of a complete mess -- you have the old Pictish traditions, overlaid by Irish invaders, then by Saxon invaders, then by the Norse, then by the English, with a lot of French in there as well courtesy of the "Auld Alliance" between France and Scotland. What parts of that tangle fed into the story of Tam Lin is an intriguing question that nobody can answer by the Queen of Elfland, and she's not talking. ;-)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: 2) It just sounds extremely likely that if, for the sake of speculation, some Pictish equivalent of the cauldron of Annwn occasionally needed a few souls stuffed back into it for whatever reason, it would be just the kind of of thing that a James I or John Knox type would hear and say, "Ach, that's obviously the Devil's Hell, because we all know the fairies are DEMONS!"

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's certainly possible, but there could be any number of other things behind it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: In Old Norse lore, Hel is a powerful Divine female being.  She presides over the realm where most folk end up after death.  (But half of those slain in battle go to Odin in Valhalla; the other half go Freya in her great hall.)   Since tithes (tiends) are mostly paid to beings, not to mere places, I suspect Norse influence here.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I think it's a safe guess that "hell" in this context is some sort of spiritual pagan otherworld that Christian authorities disapproved of.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's also possible that the story emerged after Christianity arrived, and was a product of the complex interplay of Pagan and Christian ideas that shows up all through Celtic faery lore. It's just not possible to say.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I'm becoming aware of a quiet doubtful, skeptical voice when doing my spiritual practices. I keep pushing through, but because this voice has been getting more insistent I've also been working at understanding its source, which is fear (of disorder, of choosing the wrong path, of the unknown, of being gullible or manipulated, and of being overwhelmed or losing the capacity to have discernment). So I've been working at addressing that. I notice the divided will: one part is skeptical, another realizes this skepticism is likely blocking further experiences.

Any advice? And how can skepticism be a useful tool, rather than a hindrance, when it comes occult and spiritual practice?

I am keeping at my practices, and trying to judge the results by the effects the practices have, but sometimes the doubt is paralyzing.

Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: After I wrote that, I realized a major fear is of not being able to trust my own mind, or that my mind is playing tricks on me about the experiences I am having.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's very much part of what I'm talking about.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Nearly everyone in the industrial world has been programmed from infancy on to have those doubts. The current system maintains itself by wheedling, bullying, and tricking people into ignoring all their innate capacities that don't make a profit for the institutions of the status quo. The fear you're feeling isn't yours; it's something you've been taught to feel.

The fact that you're conscious of it is excellent news; most people aren't aware of it, though it constantly shapes their thoughts, feelings, and choices. Your task now is to recognize the voice and ignore it. Treat it as empty noise, as though a parrot in the room with you was squawking corporate slogans. As you withdraw emotional energy from it, it'll fade away.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: OK, makes sense, thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I have this issue too. In my natal chart, Mercury is squaring Jupiter, so the doubt and second-guessing seem to be a part of my personality. It was reinforced by me taking the Internet Atheist rhetoric about basing one's beliefs on evidence seriously (it made my beliefs about the world brittle, vulnerable to disproof).

In the last few years, I had to abandon some of my beliefs about industrial civilization, the reliability of institutions, and materialism. So my doubt became stronger and operates at a more conscious level.

Patrick H

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Doubt is a fine quality if it's applied evenhandedly to all possible targets. I'd have few objections to the so-called skeptics if now and again they applied their skepticism to the pronouncements of scientists, or for that matter, the claims of rationalism itself. For example, what possible justification can there be to insist that the universe must behave in a way that matches the cognitive habits that natural selection has hardwired into our species of social primates on this one little planet? A little skepticism toward that display of jawdropping arrogance seems warranted!

What makes the manufactured doubt beaten into all of us so toxic is that it's one-sided. It never gets applied to the absurd claims and empty handwaving that so often prop up the status quo. It only gets applied to any idea that challenges the existing order of things, in much the same way that the only healing modalities the skeptics ever challenge are the ones that don't make profits for huge pharmaceutical corporations.

The point of all this rambling? To make your doubt conscious and apply it to the things you're not supposed to doubt is a very good thing. That way leads to genuine gnosis.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: One overlooked advantage of applying doubt evenhandedly is you're more likely to keep your head when other people are losing theirs because reality fails to conform to their ideas. Which is why the Pyrrhonists advocated a thoroughgoing skepticism as a path to ataraxia.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Merry Monday, and sorry for the macabre question: I’ve heard it said, when the body dies, the spirit exits through the eyes. Is there any info about this in occult literature?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's one I haven't heard. The occult teachings I know of say that it's not just the spirit that leaves the body; when the material body dies, the rest of the self (the etheric body, astral body, mental sheath, and soul) leave as a unit. During sleep, the subtle bodies drift a little ways outside the material body, which is why you get the perceptions of the astral plane we call "dreams."  When death happens, the subtle bodies drift further away, until there's nothing connecting them but the "silver cord" that some people see in out-of-body experience; then the silver cord breaks, and the subtle bodies go their own way. Later on (usually around three days later), the etheric body is shed in much the same way, and then the astral body dissolves slowly as the emotions and memories it contains are absorbed by the soul.

comments.title: book reviews
comments.content:
I ordered 3 books from Inner Traditions a few weeks ago, and want to share initial impressions.
1. The Cunning Farmer by Todd Elliot - I'm not quite done with my initial read-through, but I have to echo JMG's comment that this is no "fluff" piece. There is a LOT in it, starting with the philosophical foundations and building up from there.

2. The Magic of the Orphic Hymns; A New Translation for the Modern Mystic by Tamra Lucid and Ronnie Pontiac. An interesting (and easy) read, though it seems part of the manuscript must have been misplaced or lost - there is a short chapter on incense and spices, but nothing on any of the individual hymns in my copy about either of those. The authors say they found the older language of the Taylor translations to be difficult, and to grant a point it HAS been over two centuries since he translated those. According to the first chapter, Pontiac decided to go back to the original texts in ancient Greek and went word-by-word with an English-Ancient Greek dictionary, and leaving it in free form instead of fitting it into a verse structure as Taylor did.

The thing that sticks in my mind the most though, is that the authors first met when they were both interns at the PRC in the 1980s, and both got to do research under Manly P. Hall himself. (I was still in grade school at the time.)

3. The Tradition of Household Spirits: Ancestral Lore and Practices, by Claude Lecouteux; translated by Jon E. Graham. I'm a little more than halfway through, but am enjoying it immensely. Oh, and he mentioned the Russian household spirits that are being discussed in a different post this week, as well as some from Britain, Germany, the Scandinavian countries, and of course his native France. I'm sure JMG could read it in the original French, but I am glad for an English translation. I may need to pick up more of his work.

I don't have a question this week, just taking advantage of DW not mistakenly blocking me because HughesNet must have a bird that phones home to Mississippi, and I am on satellite internet down here at the dead end of my dirt road in Florida.

replies.title: Re: book reviews
replies.content: Thank you for this! I'll want to look at the last of those  -- Lecouteux is very good, in either language.

I'm quite a few years down the path now and I've recently had an experience of my reality cracking a bit. I have developed the capacity to distinguish different thought forms in my head from each other - sort of like there are different little sub-personalities. This led to a freak out about where exactly the 'real' me is - because most of them are hollow and echo old fear, habits etc that aren't mine. I was sort of aware of it before, but not to the same 'ah hah' extent.

Now I have been practicing golden dawn for a few years daily now, invoke/pray too my higher self daily (and recieve consistent energetic response in my heart space).

I'm wondering if you have any advice for truly connecting with the higher self? If there any books to read on it? How do I find that enduring essence that feels like 'me' that others speak of? What am I actually looking for?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, good. You've run up against one of the major challenges that every serious mage faces, and that's a positive thing. What can you do about it? Nothing -- and that's exactly the point.

You don't find the higher self; it finds you. You don't have the power to look for it, or find it, or make it come into contact with you. All you can do is invite its presence, over and over again...and wait. Your higher self is the active force in the relationship.

The fact that you've begun to distinguish the multiple personae in your head from yourself is an excellent sign, btw. As Zen masters like to say, you need to see the face you had before your mother and father were born, and again, you can't make that happen. Keep on with your practices, trust in the process, and it'll happen the moment you're actually ready for it.
